tax: Native Copper Coil is placed at species rank beneath Metalcoil (Metalline order, Earth Elementals class). Diagnosis rests on a native-copper ribbon body, electrical discharge through wet rock, and a verdigris trail. It is separated from its sibling the Bog-Iron Bloomling by its conductive coiling copper versus the Bloomling's inert porous iron mass; the two share the genus body-plan but never the electrical discharge. Composition, not colour or menace, fixes the placement: native copper drawn from ore-seam, kept ductile by earth-charge.
ap: Native Copper Coil stands 2–3 m unwound, a finger-thick ribbon. Its silhouette is a coiling metallic serpent that rears and lashes. Three diagnostic surface markers separate it in the field: a bright metallic gleam fading to green verdigris along older coils; a faint ozone smell and hair-raising static before it strikes; and a low hum, rising before discharge, audible in a quiet gallery. It moves with a fluid serpentine slither, fastest over wet conductive stone, and shows a faint blue arc-glint at the coil tips before discharge. Even after dispersal it can be identified from a green verdigris smear and scorched arc-marks on damp rock, so a survey team need not see the living body to log the species.
eco: Native Copper Coil persists in copper-seamed wet galleries of Tier II, where ore and standing damp let it both feed and conduct. It does not breed; it forms where earth-charge animates a native-copper seam into a coherent ribbon, and recurs whenever the charge rebuilds the ribbon from the same seam after it cools and breaks. It sustains its mass on earth-charge conducted through ore and wet stone, drawing in native copper drawn from the seam to repair and grow. Range is bounded by the conductive damp zone of its seam: outside that band the body loses charge on dry ground and falls slack. Juvenile or nascent forms appear as a copper seam that grows bright tendrils against the grain of the ore, which lets a surveyor predict where an adult will rise.
beh: Behaviour is the behaviour of its medium given intent. Native Copper Coil is roused by footfall on its wet floor or contact with metal tools and otherwise lies a coil of bright wire posing as exposed ore. It claims a single copper gallery and answers intrusion with rearing and discharging through the wet floor at anything grounded. Toward grey-folk it is reactive to metal and damp; survey crews report that Forgemasters cut its galleries with wooden tools and dry boots after losing crews to the floor-charge. It is not cultural and keeps no craft — its 'decisions' are thresholds of pressure, heat, current, or charge, not plans.
traitsText: Stable characters of Native Copper Coil: a body of native copper drawn from ore-seam, kept ductile by earth-charge; immune to electricity and to slashing, which it simply re-coils; and a defining weakness — dry insulation and heat, which cut its charge. Its senses run through the medium itself: electrical-field sensing through the wet stone; blind to dry, ungrounded objects. It tolerates acid seep and darkness but fails at dry ground and strong heat, both of which break its conduction. Mass varies with available element, yet the proportions, the ribbon body and coil-and-rear strike, stay constant, which is why mass alone never reclassifies the taxon.
abilities: Abilities of Native Copper Coil are bounded by its anatomy, not borrowed from other elements: it discharges earth-charge through wet stone to stun grounded prey; it constricts in copper coils to crush and hold; it draws copper from the seam to repair and lengthen; and it senses footfalls as field-changes through the wet floor. Each is a direct expression of earth-charge moving through a conductive copper body, so it cannot, for instance, cross dry ground, fly, or strike an insulated target. The powers scale with body mass and fade as the body disperses.
bg: In the Throat, Native Copper Coil matters because it makes copper galleries lethal to wet-booted crews, and Forgemasters now work them dry and wooden-tooled. Grey-folk survey practice treats it as an electrical hazard logged with copper seams and damp floors, and its presence reshapes routes through the Tier II copper galleries, now entered dry-shod with insulated tools. A copper seam growing bright against the ore-grain is the Forgemasters' sign a Coil is forming.
g: Native Copper Coil (Metallum cupreum) is a metalline elemental of native copper that coils as a serpentine ribbon and discharges through wet stone. Documented from the copper-seamed wet galleries of Tier II, it expresses a single inner element as its whole anatomy — a sinuous ribbon of bright and verdigris-green native copper — rather than as flesh laid over an animal skeleton. It is the only earth elemental that hunts with current rather than mass, conducting earth-charge into anything it touches. Field teams record it as a true elemental, not a beast wearing mineral armour: when it disperses it leaves a tangle of cooling copper wire and a green verdigris stain on the stone and no carcass.
